problem: RSH-358 mentions the CLI as a limitation of the database approach: 'there is a CLI that other agents can use but it's not inherently portable.' The CLI currently has no mind map commands. If storage moves to repository-based, the CLI's role in mind map access would change — agents could interact with mind maps directly via git/files rather than through CLI HTTP calls.

files.why: Main entry point. Registers top-level commands: login, token, org, tickets, inspect, comments, update. No mind map commands exist.
HTTP client (hxFetch) with retry logic and auth headers. All CLI operations go through this to reach the server API. This is the 'not inherently portable' layer the user references.
Ticket command implementations. Closest analog to what mind map commands would look like if added.
CLI binary definition (hlx), dependencies. TypeScript + Commander.js CLI framework.

facts: No mind map commands exist in the CLI — zero references to mindmap/mind-map in the command registry.
The CLI communicates with the server exclusively via HTTP REST API (hxFetch with X-API-Key or Bearer auth).
The ticket explicitly calls out the CLI as not 'inherently portable' — agents must go through Helix's API to interact with data.
The CLI supports: tickets (CRUD, rerun, continue, artifacts, bundle), inspect (db, logs, api), comments, org management, auth.
With repository-based storage, agents could bypass the CLI entirely and work with mind map files directly via git operations.
unknowns: Whether the CLI would still need mind map commands if storage is repository-based (agents could use git directly).
Whether a CLI mind map command set was planned for the database approach in RSH-353 (no evidence found).
What 'portability' means in the CLI context: format portability, access portability, or independence from Helix infrastructure.
